revision.text:
'''万寿森古墳'''（まんじゅもりこふん）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]][[湯村]]三丁目の所在する[[古墳]]<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。年代は[[6世紀]]中葉に推定されており、形式は[[円墳]]<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
== 立地と地理的・歴史的景観 == [[甲府盆地]]の北縁に位置し、湯村山南の山裾に立地する<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。[[標高]]は300メートル付近<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
[[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]東岸の甲府市湯村・[[千塚 (甲府市)|千塚]]・[[羽黒町 (甲府市)|羽黒町]]・山宮町一帯は古くから[[千塚・山宮古墳群]]と呼ばれる古墳群が濃密に分布する地域として知られる<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。まず千塚の榎田遺跡が[[弥生時代]]から[[平安時代]]の集落遺跡として知られ、弥生時代の[[方形周溝墓]]が検出されており、古墳後期に最盛期を迎える<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.355</ref>。
万寿森古墳の東には[[笛吹市]]御坂町井ノ上の[[姥塚古墳]]に次ぐ山梨県下に第二位の規模である6世紀後半の[[加牟那塚古墳]]（かむなづかこふん）が所在している<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357、p.47</ref>。ほか、羽黒の[[天狗山古墳]]は6世紀代に築造された[[積石塚]]の円墳で、甲府盆地北縁に分布する[[渡来人]]墓制である積石塚の西端に位置づけられている<ref>田代・萩原（1984）、p.48</ref>。湯村山西麓には大平古墳群が所在している<ref>田代・萩原（1984）、p.48</ref>。
万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが<ref>『加牟那塚』、p.2</ref>、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している<ref>田代・萩原（1984）、p.48</ref>。
== 万寿森古墳の規模と築造年代 == 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。<ref>『加牟那塚』、p.</ref>石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。主軸は南北<ref>田代・萩原（1984）、p.48</ref>。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
[[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている<ref>『山梨県の地名』、p.357</ref>。
== 脚注 == <references />
== 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年
{{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]]

万寿森古墳（2015年8月12日撮影） |所在地=山梨県甲府市千塚3丁目 |位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19|scale:10000}} |形状=円墳 |規模=東西31m、南北38m、高さ5m |築造年代=6世紀中葉 |埋葬施設= |被葬者= |出土品= |史跡指定=市史跡 |特記事項= }} '''万寿森古墳'''（まんじゅもりこふん）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]][[湯村]]三丁目の所在する[[古墳]]『山梨県の地名』、p.357。年代は[[6世紀]]中葉に推定されており、形式は[[円墳]]『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 == 立地と地理的・歴史的景観 == [[甲府盆地]]の北縁に位置し、湯村山南の山裾に立地する『山梨県の地名』、p.357。[[標高]]は300メートル付近『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]東岸の甲府市湯村・[[千塚 (甲府市)|千塚]]・[[羽黒町 (甲府市)|羽黒町]]・山宮町一帯は古くから[[千塚・山宮古墳群]]と呼ばれる古墳群が濃密に分布する地域として知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。まず千塚の榎田遺跡が[[弥生時代]]から[[平安時代]]の集落遺跡として知られ、弥生時代の[[方形周溝墓]]が検出されており、古墳後期に最盛期を迎える『山梨県の地名』、p.355。 万寿森古墳の西北西には[[笛吹市]]御坂町井ノ上の[[姥塚古墳]]に次ぐ山梨県下に第二位の規模である6世紀後半の[[加牟那塚古墳]]（かむなづかこふん）が所在している『山梨県の地名』、p.357、p.47。ほか、羽黒の[[天狗山古墳]]は6世紀代に築造された[[積石塚]]の円墳で、甲府盆地北縁に分布する[[渡来人]]墓制である積石塚の西端に位置づけられている田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。湯村山西麓には大平古墳群が所在している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。古代の[[律令制]]下では[[巨摩郡]]に含まれる。 万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが『加牟那塚』、p.2、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。 == 万寿森古墳の規模と築造年代 == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|大正初期頃に撮影された万寿森古墳。南西側より。今日は宅地化が進み、このような角度からの撮影はできない。]] 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）『山梨県の地名』、p.357。『加牟那塚』、p.石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主軸は南北田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年 {{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]]

[[甲府盆地]]の北縁に位置し、湯村山南の山裾に立地する『山梨県の地名』、p.357。[[標高]]は300メートル付近『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]東岸の甲府市湯村・[[千塚 (甲府市)|千塚]]・[[羽黒町 (甲府市)|羽黒町]]・山宮町一帯は古くから[[千塚・山宮古墳群]]と呼ばれる古墳群が濃密に分布する地域として知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。まず千塚の榎田遺跡が[[弥生時代]]から[[平安時代]]の集落遺跡として知られ、弥生時代の[[方形周溝墓]]が検出されており、古墳後期に最盛期を迎える『山梨県の地名』、p.355。 万寿森古墳の西北西に所在する[[加牟那塚古墳]]（かむなづかこふん）は6世紀後半の築造で、[[笛吹市]]御坂町井ノ上の[[姥塚古墳]]に次ぐ山梨県下に第二位の規模の古墳であることで知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357、p.47。ほか、羽黒町の[[天狗山古墳]]は6世紀代に築造された[[積石塚]]の円墳で、甲府盆地北縁に分布する[[渡来人]]墓制である積石塚の西端に位置づけられている田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。湯村山西麓には大平古墳群が所在している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。古代の[[律令制]]下では[[巨摩郡]]に含まれる。 万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが『加牟那塚』、p.2、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。 == 万寿森古墳の規模と築造年代 == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|大正初期頃に撮影された万寿森古墳。南西側より。今日は宅地化が進み、このような角度からの撮影はできない。]] 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）『山梨県の地名』、p.357。『加牟那塚』、p.石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主軸は南北田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年 {{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]]

山梨県公報第2582号]}}。 == 立地と地理的・歴史的景観 == [[甲府盆地]]の北縁に位置し、[[湯村山]]南の山裾に立地する『山梨県の地名』、p.357。[[標高]]は300メートル付近『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]東岸の甲府市湯村・[[千塚 (甲府市)|千塚]]・[[羽黒町 (甲府市)|羽黒町]]・山宮町一帯は古くから[[千塚・山宮古墳群]]と呼ばれる古墳群が濃密に分布する地域として知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。まず千塚の榎田遺跡が[[弥生時代]]から[[平安時代]]の集落遺跡として知られ、弥生時代の[[方形周溝墓]]が検出されており、古墳後期に最盛期を迎える『山梨県の地名』、p.355。 万寿森古墳の西北西に所在する[[加牟那塚古墳]]（かむなづかこふん）は6世紀後半の築造で、[[笛吹市]]御坂町井ノ上の[[姥塚古墳]]に次ぐ山梨県下に第二位の規模の古墳であることで知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357、p.47。ほか、羽黒町の[[天狗山古墳]]は6世紀代に築造された[[積石塚]]の円墳で、甲府盆地北縁に分布する[[渡来人]]墓制である積石塚の西端に位置づけられている田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。湯村山西麓には大平古墳群が所在している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。古代の[[律令制]]下では[[巨摩郡]]に含まれる。 万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが『加牟那塚』、p.2、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。 == 万寿森古墳の規模と築造年代 == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|大正初期頃に撮影された万寿森古墳。南西側より。今日は宅地化が進み、このような角度からの撮影はできない。]] 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）『山梨県の地名』、p.357『加牟那塚』、p.2。石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主軸は南北田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年 {{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]] [[Category:山梨県指定史跡]]

山梨県公報第2582号]}}。 == 立地と地理的・歴史的景観 == [[甲府盆地]]の北縁に位置し、[[湯村山]]南の山裾に立地する『山梨県の地名』、p.357。[[標高]]は300メートル付近『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[荒川 (山梨県)|荒川]]東岸の甲府市湯村・[[千塚 (甲府市)|千塚]]・[[羽黒町 万寿森古墳の西北西に所在する[[加牟那塚古墳]]（かむなづかこふん）は6世紀後半の築造で、[[笛吹市]]御坂町井ノ上の[[姥塚古墳]]に次ぐ山梨県下に第二位の規模の古墳であることで知られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357、p.47。ほか、羽黒町の[[天狗山古墳]]は6世紀代に築造された[[積石塚]]の円墳で、甲府盆地北縁に分布する[[渡来人]]墓制である積石塚の西端に位置づけられている田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。湯村山西麓には大平古墳群が所在している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。古代の[[律令制]]下では[[巨摩郡]]に含まれる。 万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが『加牟那塚』、p.2、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。 == 万寿森古墳の規模と築造年代 == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|大正初期頃に撮影された万寿森古墳。南西側より。今日は宅地化が進み、このような角度からの撮影はできない。]] 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）『山梨県の地名』、p.357『加牟那塚』、p.2。石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。主軸は南北田代・萩原（1984）、p.48。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル『山梨県の地名』、p.357。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 [[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた『山梨県の地名』、p.357。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている『山梨県の地名』、p.357。 == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年 {{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]] [[Category:山梨県指定史跡]]

万寿森古墳は湯村山南麓に分布する湯村山古墳群のひとつで、万寿森古墳のほか10数基の古墳が確認されているが『加牟那塚』、p.2、これらの古墳群は多くが開発により消失している。 == 規模と築造年代 == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|大正初期頃に撮影された万寿森古墳。南西側より。今日は宅地化が進み、このような角度からの撮影はできない。]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|石室 玄室}}]] 万寿森古墳の規模は東西31メートル、南北38メートル、高さ5メートル。主体部の保存状態は良好であるが、後述の通り[[江戸時代]]から[[石室]]を利用された痕跡が見られる。主体部は南に開口する両袖形の[[横穴式石室]]で、[[安山岩]]の[[自然石]]による乱石積み（小口積み・横口積み）。石室の部材は50センチから80センチの比較的小型の石材が使われている点が指摘される。 石室の全長は14.2メートル、玄室長7.9メートル、羨道長5.3メートル。主軸は南北。石室幅は奥壁付近で2.44メートル、玄室部の袖側で1.61メートル、羨道入口で1.55メートル。高さは後述の通り[[床|床面]]が後代に[[コンクリート]]舗装されているため正確には不明であるが、最大で3.3メートルを測っており、数10センチの幅が想定されている。 [[副葬品]]や[[埴輪]]・[[葺石]]・[[周溝]]など外部施設は知られておらず、正確な築造年代も不明。近在の甲府市千塚の加牟那塚古墳や、笛吹市御坂町の姥塚古墳では大型石材が用いられているため、万寿森古墳はこれに先行する築造であるとも考えられている。 江戸後期の『[[甲斐国志]]』によれば万寿森古墳は古くから存在を知られており、江戸時代には石室を[[煙硝]]の保管庫として利用し「煙硝蔵」と称されていた。近代にも[[ホテル]]の倉庫として利用されており、開口部には[[扉]]が取り付けられ、床面にはコンクリートが敷かれている。 Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|玄室（開口部方向） Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|羨道（開口部方向） Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|羨道（玄室方向） Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|開口部 Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|墳丘・石室開口部（2015年） == 脚注 == == 参考文献 == * 『日本歴史地名大系19 山梨県の地名』[[平凡社]]、[[1995年]] * 田代孝・萩原三男「甲府市域の古墳分布とニ、三の課題」『甲府市史研究 創刊号』1984年 * 石神孝子「万寿森古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まんしゆもりこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:円墳]] [[Category:山梨県指定史跡]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Japanese burial mound | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == Location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[ja:万寿森古墳]]

The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:万寿森古墳]]

The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. {{Infobox Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[ja:万寿森古墳]]

File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. revision.text:
{{Infobox Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round tumulus|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] 万寿森古墳]]

Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. revision.text:
{{Infobox Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:kk]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:kk]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:kk]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:kk]][[ja:万寿森古墳]]

It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:万寿森古墳]]

It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place Names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place Names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:万寿森古墳]]

The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). {{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round tumuli]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:万寿森古墳]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Round tumuli]] to [[Category:Round Kofun]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kofu]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Sites]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]][[category:Manjumori Kofun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:万寿森古墳]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-2.jpg|Main chamber, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-2.jpg|Passage, view towards entrance File:Manjumori Kofun, sendou-1.jpg|Passage, view towards main chamber File:Manjumori Kofun, entrance.jpg|Entrance File:Manjumori Kofun Aug. 2015.JPG|Tumulus and stone chamber entrance (2015) == References == == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995}} * Tashiro, Ko & Hagiwara, Mitsuo. "Distribution of Kofun in Kofu City and Related Issues." *Kofu City History Research* Vol. 1, 1984. * Ishigami, Takako. "Manjumori Kofun." *Yamanashi Prefecture History, Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998. == External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Manjumori Kofun}} Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Manjumori Kofun | image = [[File:Manjumori Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Tumulus and stone chamber entrance | location = {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura 3-chome | coordinates = {{Coord|35|40|50.0|N|138|33|8.4|E|region:JP-19_scale:10000}} | type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳}} | dimensions = East-west: 31m, North-south: 38m, Height: 5m | period = Mid-6th century | burial_facility = | burial_individual = | artifacts = | designation = Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Historic Site }} '''Manjumori Kofun''' (万寿森古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Yumura. It has been designated as a Yamanashi Prefecture Historic Site{{PDFlink|[https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gyousei-kk/kouhou/documents/2582.pdf Yamanashi Prefecture Official Gazette No. 2582]}}. == location and Geographic-Historical Landscape == The tumulus is situated at the northern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, on the southern foothills of {{ill|Mount Yumura|ja|湯村山}}, at an elevation of approximately 300 meters* {{Cite book|title=Yamanashi Prefecture Place names|publisher=Heibonsha|year=1995|page=357}}. The surrounding area, including Yumura, Chizuka, Haguro, and Yamamiya neighborhoods, is known for the {{ill|Chizuka-Yamamiya Kofun Cluster|ja|千塚・山宮古墳群}}, which features a high concentration of burial mounds. Notable sites include the Enokida ruins in Chizuka, which span from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, and feature remains such as {{ill|Burial Practices of the Yayoi Period|lt=rectangular moat tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} from the Yayoi periodYamanashi Prefecture Place names, p.355. Nearby kofun include the {{ill|Kamuna Tumulus|ja|加牟那塚古墳}}, built in the late 6th century and the second largest tumulus in Yamanashi Prefecture, and the {{ill|Otengu-san Tumulus|ja|おてんぐさん古墳}}, a 6th-century stone-piled circular mound. These sites are part of the broader landscape associated with immigrant burial traditionsTashiro and Hagiwara (1984), p.48. The Manjumori Kofun is one of several mounds in the Yumura Mountain cluster, many of which have been lost to modern development. == Structure and Construction Period == [[File:Manjumori ancient burial mound in 1910 's.jpg|thumb|250px|Manjumori Kofun, circa early Taisho period.]] [[File:Manjumori Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main Chamber (玄室)}}]] The tumulus measures 31 meters east-west, 38 meters north-south, and 5 meters in height. The burial facility is a south-facing {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} constructed with andesite fieldstones. The chamber features "random stone stacking" (小口積み・横口積み) with smaller stones (50-80 cm). The chamber is 14.2 meters long, with a main chamber (玄室) measuring 7.9 meters and a passage (羨道) of 5.3 meters. Chamber width varies, being 2.44 meters near the rear wall and narrowing to 1.55 meters at the entrance. The chamber height reaches a maximum of 3.3 meters but is partially obscured by later concrete paving. Although no artifacts, haniwa, or surrounding facilities have been found, comparisons with nearby kofun suggest an earlier construction date relative to the large stone-built mounds in the region. The tumulus has been known since the Edo period and was repurposed as a storage facility for gunpowder (煙硝蔵). In modern times, it served as a hotel warehouse, with the addition of doors and concrete flooring.
